item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
this article is a great highlight of one of the problems of current community policy on how articles should be written: that it's dissonant from how an actual SCP Foundation would write them.

Any researcher would be able to put together that this is likely where 173 came from just as easily as any of us did, and so would have no reason not to flat-out say as much in the actual description.

That said, my gripe is with the policy that the article must adhere to, not the article itself. as for the article itself, it addresses one of the issues that 173's author would've been told to edit in if they submitted 173 today (which is that it completely fails both to hint at the origin of the anomaly and relate how the anomaly came under Foundation custody) and for that I commend you, but it only pushes the question a step back. no vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If you think "rubbing into face while screaming in Japanese" is subtle, then sure…

item.title: Re: +200
item.content:encoded: Splendid. I hope to revisit the DoA some day!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I would upvote but it's at +173, and i'm not breaking that
Outstanding!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is amazing. The origins of 173 revealed, with a subtle hint at something much bigger going on. Enthusiastic +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: fair game to cover it with concrete

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What I like about this one, is that it adds an sense of origin to 173 without actually giving it an origin. All this tells us is that at some point it was contain in Japan and since then have escaped, and that it is not the only one, but it doesn't specify if all of them share 173's murderous nature or even hints at their origin.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My personal theory is that 3693 is the watchman,thus theoretically capable of viewing all the statues at once. The statues don't neutralize each other. Maybe 173 noticed it wasn't being watched.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Trust me to mix the numbers up. I meant SCP-3693.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a connection between 3690 and 173?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this is playing an identical game to SCP-3693 (allusively harking back to the spartan creepypasta of the very early days while playfully positing an obscure connection with SCP-173 to make that subtext literal), but there's just not as much to it as 3690. There's a certain frisson connecting to abandoned places and hidden secrets that this taps into, and the sparseness helps that, but I can't help but feeling that this is missing something. If there was some element that hinted at what made 173 different, what gave it the ability to escape the trap, or just some extra, evocative detail that took this beyond 'abandoned cell for multiple 173's', I could see myself going for this, but as it is it's just not quite enough to overcome the aforementioned similarities to 3690, which is the fresher and more expansive concept of the two to my mind.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Highly atmospheric and easily digestible. This is everything a good SCP should aspire to be.
And in just four days. A new personal best! I'm very excited about the DOA as a concept, and I hope to write more about them!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I +1'd this for being short, to the point, but still packing lots of interest in in that space. I do have one problem. Red liquid. Why does it always have to be red liquid? Like half of all SCPs are covered in/secreting/gushing blood or blood-like fluids. You could have just said "A viscous fluid" or something and it would have been perfect for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If I’ve said it once I’ve said it a thousand times: my favorite articles are the short and sweet, walk-up-drop-a-bomb-and-walk-away articles. This left me with a big smile on my face and a word that is, from me, the highest praise:

Neat.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like the Department of Silly Walks, except for SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
subtle

okay

item.title: Surprise Pillow Fight?
item.content:encoded:
Either something screwed up, or that's an abstract title. Either way, I did enjoy this. A continuation of an already great article, we need more of those.

I do feel that this only re-established what we know about the original, and didn't really add anything new. However, it's still a +1 from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What is the Department of Abnormalities? And why does it sound so incredibly British?

item.title: "Safe"
item.content:encoded:
The containment procedures on these DOA articles really do give the impression of someone important saying: "NOTHING TO SEE HERE, MOVE ALONG"

An army of euclids and what is ostensibly a vault full of barely contained anomalies with unexplored properties do not constitute "safe" classification

…something's going on here

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this best expresses my thoughts. It's just sort of a mashup of 3693 with the Department of Abnormalities (is that going to become a thing now?) When we're on the cusp of Series 5, I have to ask why we're looking back and trying to explain where 173 came from.
Nice! And in just 13 hours! That’s a new personal best!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
2) there’s no barrier. The fronts of the cells are exposed so that every sculpture is in full view.

3) I’m glad you did the math, haha - I sure wasn’t going to.

4) Yes, the assumption is that the cell is empty because 173 escaped from it, isn’t it?

5) There sure are a lot of corpses with broken necks on Hashimi Island, aren’t there?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ lmao spoilers
- Hide

I like how you connected SCP-3693 with that of SCP-3790. It really adds a great deal of depth, mystery, intrigue, and world building to the whole piece.

I have a few ideas of how we could connect the origin of 173 from 3693 and to this one, but for the sake of letting the article speak for itself, I'll just let it sink in.

Does this mean we get a Department of Abnormalities Canon hub?

ETA: I didn't mean to reply to you, Chandra.

item.title: ARTICLE UPDATES
item.content:encoded: I decided to move one of the crosslinks to the end of the article; in its former position it seemed to be spoiling the article entirely. This new location maintains the story connection while managing to actually deepen the mystery.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The two major changes I suggested for this was removing all concrete references to 173 (even taking out the reference to fecal matter) and linking it to the Department of Abnormalities. My reasoning was thus: I think this works better if you don't think of it as 173. It's just something very, very similar.

Re: Nothing being anomalous here, this actually fits neatly into one of my philosophies regarding SCPs; I don't think you actually need to tell us what the anomaly is. SCPs are procedures primarily for containment, not for explaining the anomaly.

For me, a major takeaway of this article is that whatever the anomaly is down there, the Foundation doesn't want to talk about it. They just want to seal it with concrete and forget that it exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So I have a few questions about the mechanics of this panoptical silo.

1) So the implication here is that 173 and his species freeze not only when observed but so long as they believe they might be observed. If this is the case, then why is it only the top ten floors that have an observation room? Most of the lower 190 floors would be sufficiently obstructed from the top of the watchtower.

Wait, I just had a thought: these things are like Weeping Angels, and can't even move when they're being watched by each other. That makes much more sense.

2) So is there any kind of barrier keeping the Crunchies inside the cells? I can understand the reasoning for this, since they could smear their blood and fecal matter over a transparent barrier to obscured themselves, but then if they think they're/are being watched at all times then they shouldn't be able to move anyways.

3) Assuming the twenty-meter diameter is approximate and the 3-meter cell length includes the thickness of the walls, there are twenty-one cells per floor. That means there are 4199 Crunchies in this tower.

4) I know you're not going to answer this, but assuming they can't move under each other's gaze, how did 173 make it to the tower, break the guards neck, and then escape the silo? Is he a mutant, immune to the effects of his brethern?

5) How does Salty (3693) fit into this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I downvoted basically for this reason. I don't get anything out of this other than the initial presentation of an admittedly interesting concept. 3693 was better because the links to 173 weren't laid out in your face right from the start of the article, and it left some things to the imagination as to how it could be related to 173 and the backstory of the two objects (is 173 the lover? is it something else?). This simply presents the 173 backstory as an idea and doesn't take it anywhere. I mean, you could exercise your imagination as a reader to construct a story around this in the same way as 3693, but the most boring, simple explanation for the backstory that doesn't tell you anything new about 173 ('this is where it was previously contained by the not-Foundation, then it got out') is the story that immediately springs to mind, even if that wasn't the author's intention.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought it was supposed to be the guy in the watch-tower thing was doing it? And yeah this'd be anomalous since it's related to the department of abnormalities. Presumably it also has no clear origin but the page doesn't really say.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really dig it!

Short, and sweet, I think my favorite takeaways are:

1) SCP-173 is sentient enough to be able to be tricked by a Panopticon.
2) SCP-173's abilities are implied less to be automatic, and more something it thinks about before enacting
3) Holy hell, someone mass produced them.

Awesome job, ARD. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm really split on this, to the point that I first downvoted, then upvoted, and may still change my mind further.

The concept is clever and I like the tie ins, but honestly the tie ins feel like all this is. 3693 already did the "173 backstory as skip" thing, but it did it by developing a unique object that revealed info backwards. This is just a bunch of 173s a la revised entry containing each other a la roget's proposal. Come to think of it, nothing in this article even says this is anomalous. It just says the building is there, and implies the statues are anomalous, in which case why aren't they the skip when the building is just a building? Idk man, I think I just talked myself into downvoting again. I think this concept would make a better tale than a skip tbh.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There's a lot to like here but I think my favourite thing about it is that it makes the Department of Abnormalities more of a thing. I can see getting a lot of mileage out of that concept.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel like this would work better without the hints at the beginning so the ending is more of an "oh shit that's what going on here", If that's what you were trying to anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: finally, an article that caters to my attention span

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you for my new headcanon, guys.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree on the headcanon aspect - I brought it up during my crit and it still almost earned a no vote because of it. But I upvoted for the same reasons

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow, subtle reference to 173, and an interesting one at that!

Sir, enjoy your +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't actually find this terribly clever. I actually greatly dislike the headcanon presented here; that there are many 173s and that one escaped - OURS!

But holy shit I love how this is structured and written. It's brief but not too brief, it's ingenious how its such a simple concept I never thought of, and fuck it gets everything necessary down while allowing the reader to imagine more.

Just imagining 173 scrambling up the tube, bashing it with its limbs, and voiping away to become a loosed horror is awesome.

+1, big fan even if I loathe the headcanon. Awesome work ARD. More like this!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I went "this is awfully short."

Now I go "this is awfully good."

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very, very clever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The original draft that I saw lacked layers that made this interesting outside of the obvious intent of the article seen at the end - this one has a bunch more that make it interesting. I won’t say more to avoid spoiling for anyone that hasn’t gotten it yet.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
173 origin? Escaped prisoner statue.

Whilst not my headcanon, it's clever and well written.

+1

item.title: No fixed solution
item.content:encoded:
Been a long time since I managed to write an article in less than a day. It's good to be back.

Many thanks to Mortos, Kalinin, and taytay1677 for their feedback.
Special thanks to The Great Hippo for his critique, and to Jacob Conwell and ChaoSera for their input on the ending.

See more of my work on my author page!
